description: All 40 benchmarks passing. Changes: Initial system build: 4 generators, 3 validators, error taxonomy, benchmark framework, gold bank. Fixed hint_leaks_answer in fraction L4 and average L2 generators.

All 120 benchmarks passing. Changes: Phase 1: expand benchmarks 40->120 (30 per topic). Added metadata: pattern_type (normal/edge/boundary/adversarial/wording_variation), risk_level (low/medium/high), source_reason. Discovered hint leak vulnerabilities: (1) unit_conversion value=1 forward causes answer=multiplier leak, (2) decimal answer substring in operand strings. Documented but not fixed (generator changes deferred).
Hint leak vulnerability: unit_conversion with value=1 forward produces answer=multiplier, which always appears in L2/L3 hints. Affects all 9 conversion pairs. Requires generator-level fix.
Hint leak vulnerability: answer can be substring of operand in hint. E.g. answer 50 in 50.0, answer 100 in 1000. Requires smarter hint leak detection ignoring substrings within larger numbers.
Wording inconsistency: average template_index=0 hardcodes san-ci-kao-shi but accepts any value count. Some cases pass non-3 values, creating misleading problem text.

Fraction mutation survival pattern: boundary nudge (den +/- 1) often creates input where numerator is divisible by new denominator, making the fraction simplify to a whole number (not a real fraction problem). Output-based checks (verifying generator output params, not just mutation input) are needed to catch these. LCD complexity check also effective for large coprime denominators.

Equivalent mutations are a significant fraction of survivors (~24/63). These are mutations that produce the exact same answer as the original (e.g. swap_ab for commutative operations, template_shift for wording-only templates). Detecting these as a separate category gives a more accurate mutation kill rate and reveals which mutations are truly testing generator robustness vs just producing equivalent problems.

Mutation testing natural plateau: remaining 39 survivors (32.5%%) are mostly genuinely valid alternative problems (different template wording, different denominators producing valid fractions, decimal conversions that are grade-appropriate). Further kill rate improvement requires either (1) detecting difficulty drift (mutated problem significantly easier/harder than original) or (2) tighter pedagogical constraints that may conflict with existing benchmarks. Average_word_problem has reached 100%% as the first topic.

Difficulty drift detection is highly effective for catching pedagogically inappropriate mutations. Fraction problems with same denominators (LCD=den) are especially vulnerable to large LCD drift when den is nudged (e.g., a_den=4,b_den=4 -> b_den=5 creates LCD=20, a 5x drift). The 3x threshold is conservative enough to avoid false positives while catching 11 additional mutations. The approach is cross-cutting: a single difficulty estimator per topic catches drift across different mutation operators. Fraction benefit was largest (+22.5%%) due to many same-denominator benchmark cases. Total kill rate broke through the plateau from 67.5%% to 76.7%%.

Expanding mutation operators to test previously untested parameter dimensions (numerators for fractions) is high-leverage even when existing checks are mature. All 15 new numerator mutations were killed because: (1) zero-numerator makes answer trivially easy AND triggers L3 hint leaks (answer equals the non-zero operand, which appears in L3 setup text), (2) numerator-equals-denominator simplifies to integer (caught by existing den=1 check). This shows that mutation testing compounds by expanding surface area, not just tightening quality checks on existing mutations.

Grade-appropriateness checks on answer format (not just value) are highly effective for unit conversion mutations. Direction flip and decimal value mutations often produce valid arithmetic but pedagogically inappropriate answers (too many decimal places for elementary students). The >2 decimal places threshold is conservative and catches reverse conversions producing 0.003, 0.004, 0.0005 etc. while allowing valid decimal answers like 2.5, 0.25. This single check killed 7 mutations across 3 different mutation operators (direction_flip, value_huge, decimal_value), showing that format-level quality checks have cross-cutting impact similar to difficulty drift detection.
